安.note: A woman sheltered under a roof — in ancient China this image embodied total peace and security for the household. The character captures the idea that safety begins at home.

明.note: Sun and moon together — the two great sources of natural light. Their union creates the concept of brightness, clarity, and understanding itself.

好.note: A woman beside a child — the ancient image of goodness, harmony, and completion. Some scholars also read it as the joy of new life.

休.note: A person leaning against a tree — the universal image of rest. This pictographic logic has been unchanged for over three thousand years.

男.note: Strength applied to the field. In the agrarian civilization of ancient China, a man was defined by his labour in the paddy fields — the character encodes this social role directly.

radicals.meaning: the character itself is a radical

木.note: One of the oldest pictographs — a tree with roots below and branches above. The radical 木 seeds hundreds of characters related to trees, wood, and growth.

radicals.meaning: the character itself is a radical

日.note: Originally drawn as a circle with a dot — the sun and its core. The dot distinguishes it from 口 (mouth). 日 seeds all characters related to time, light, and heat.

radicals.meaning: the character itself is a radical

月.note: The crescent moon — one of the most stable pictographs in Chinese. 月 also doubles as the radical for 肉 (flesh/meat), leading to many body-part characters.

radicals.meaning: the character itself is a radical

山.note: Three mountain peaks rendered as brushstrokes — a direct sketch of the landscape. 山 appears in place names, nature poetry, and spiritual concepts of stillness and permanence.

radicals.meaning: the character itself is a radical

水.note: Flowing water shown as a central current with side streams. As the radical 氵 it influences hundreds of characters related to rivers, liquids, and states of flow.

radicals.meaning: the character itself is a radical

火.note: Flames rising — a direct pictograph of fire. As a radical (灬 or 火) it appears in characters for cooking, heat, light, and passion.

学.note: A child under a sheltering structure — learning happens in a protected space, nurtured and guided. The traditional form 學 includes hands passing knowledge downward.

radicals.meaning: the character itself is a radical

人.note: A standing person seen from the side, legs apart. One of the oldest and most productive pictographs — 人 or 亻 appears in countless characters involving human beings, roles, and relationships.

radicals.meaning: the character itself is a radical

大.note: A person with arms spread wide — embodying largeness, greatness, and abundance. The same figure with a dot above becomes 天 (sky/heaven), and a dot below becomes 太 (great/excessive).

radicals.meaning: the character itself is a radical

心.note: A pictograph of the physical heart — in Chinese philosophy the heart is also the seat of mind, emotion, and consciousness. As radical 忄 it seeds all characters involving feeling and thought.

radicals.meaning: the character itself is a radical

口.note: An open mouth — simple, direct, and enormously productive. 口 appears in characters for speaking, eating, singing, and calling. It also represents openings, gates, and entrances by extension.

时.note: The sun measured in small increments — time itself. The traditional form 時 pairs 日 with 寺 (temple), a place where time was marked by bells. The simplified form keeps the essence.

友.note: Two right hands reaching toward each other — a handshake across time. Friendship in the oracle bone script was literally two hands meeting. The gesture defines the relationship.

国.note: A treasure (jade, or a weapon in older forms) enclosed within a border — a nation is defined by what it protects within its boundaries. The simplified form uses 玉 (jade), the most precious material in Chinese civilization.

radicals.meaning: the character itself is a radical

中.note: A vertical line piercing the center of a rectangle — an arrow hitting the bullseye. 中 means middle, center, and China itself (中国, the Middle Kingdom), the country that considered itself the center of the world.
